seoTitle: Hangshun MCU Products | ARM Cortex-M Microcontrollers | BeiLuo Distributor
seoDescription: Browse Hangshun 32-bit ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers. General purpose, touch control, motor control, and wireless MCUs with technical specifications and selection guide.

faqs.question: What MCU categories does Hangshun offer?
faqs.answer: Hangshun offers four main MCU categories designed for different applications: 1) General Purpose MCU - versatile 32-bit ARM Cortex-M0/M3/M4 microcontrollers for embedded systems, industrial control, and consumer electronics; 2) Touch Control MCU - integrated capacitive touch sensing with up to 24 channels, water rejection technology, and low power consumption for HMI applications; 3) Motor Control MCU - dedicated MCUs with integrated gate drivers, advanced PWM units, and hardware acceleration for BLDC, PMSM, and stepper motor control; 4) Wireless MCU - BLE 5.0 and Sub-1GHz connectivity solutions for IoT applications with ultra-low power consumption. Each category is optimized for specific use cases while maintaining the common ARM ecosystem compatibility.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select General Purpose for standard embedded applications, Touch Control for HMI interfaces, Motor Control for drives, or Wireless for IoT connectivity.

faqs.question: How do I choose between Hangshun MCU categories?
faqs.answer: Choosing the right Hangshun MCU category depends on your application requirements: 1) General Purpose MCU - choose when you need standard processing with rich peripherals (UART, SPI, I2C, ADC, timers) without specialized features; suitable for industrial control, data acquisition, and general embedded systems; 2) Touch Control MCU - select when your application requires capacitive touch interface (buttons, sliders, wheels); ideal for home appliances, consumer electronics, and industrial panels; includes water rejection for kitchen applications; 3) Motor Control MCU - required for motor drive applications (BLDC, PMSM, stepper); features integrated drivers and control algorithms; perfect for fans, pumps, power tools, and servo systems; 4) Wireless MCU - essential for IoT applications requiring BLE 5.0 or Sub-1G connectivity; best for smart home, wireless sensors, and remote monitoring. Consider your primary function first, then evaluate peripheral requirements and power constraints.
faqs.decisionGuide: Identify your primary application need (general processing, touch interface, motor control, or wireless), then browse the corresponding category for specific models.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Cortex-M0, M3, and M4 cores in Hangshun MCUs?
faqs.answer: Hangshun MCUs use three ARM Cortex-M core variants with different performance characteristics: 1) Cortex-M0 - entry-level 32-bit core, up to 48MHz, 0.9 DMIPS/MHz, simplest architecture with smallest code footprint, ideal for cost-sensitive applications with basic processing needs; 2) Cortex-M3 - mainstream performance core, up to 72MHz, 1.25 DMIPS/MHz, includes hardware divide and single-cycle multiply, better interrupt handling with NVIC, suitable for medium-complexity applications requiring real-time response; 3) Cortex-M4 - high-performance core with DSP capabilities, up to 168MHz, 1.25 DMIPS/MHz, includes single-precision FPU, DSP instructions (SIMD, MAC), and advanced debug features, perfect for applications requiring signal processing, complex algorithms, or floating-point calculations. Power consumption increases with performance: M0 < M3 < M4. Price follows the same trend. Select based on your processing requirements, algorithm complexity, and power budget.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose M0 for simple control tasks, M3 for mainstream applications, M4 for DSP and complex algorithms requiring floating-point operations.

faqs.question: Which Hangshun MCU category is best for home appliance applications?
faqs.answer: Home appliance applications typically require a combination of Hangshun MCU categories: 1) Touch Control MCU - ideal for control panels with capacitive touch buttons and displays; water rejection features are essential for washing machines and kitchen appliances; supports overlay materials up to 5mm thick; 2) Motor Control MCU - required for appliances with motors (washing machine drums, refrigerator compressors, air conditioner fans, vacuum cleaners); integrated algorithms simplify development; 3) General Purpose MCU - suitable for simpler appliances without motors or touch interfaces (microwave ovens, rice cookers); cost-effective solution with sufficient peripherals. Many modern appliances use multiple MCUs: Touch MCU for the user interface, Motor Control MCU for the drive system, and General Purpose MCU for system management. Wireless MCUs are increasingly used for smart home connectivity and remote monitoring.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Touch Control MCU for panels, Motor Control MCU for drives, and General Purpose MCU for system control in home appliances.

faqs.question: What are the key features of Hangshun Wireless MCUs?
faqs.answer: Hangshun Wireless MCUs combine microcontroller and RF connectivity in a single chip: 1) BLE 5.0 variants - support 2Mbps high-speed mode, long range (Coded PHY with 4x range), extended advertising, and multiple simultaneous connections; TX power adjustable from -20dBm to +8dBm; RX sensitivity -95dBm; 2) Sub-1G variants - operate at 433/868/915MHz with -120dBm sensitivity and +20dBm TX power; better penetration through walls and longer range than 2.4GHz; 3) Ultra-low power - BLE: 3mA RX, 4mA TX, <1μA sleep with RAM retention; Sub-1G: 10mA RX, 25mA TX at +10dBm; 4) Integrated protocol stacks - BLE peripheral/central/broadcaster/observer roles, proprietary RF protocols; 5) Coexistence features - integrated balun, antenna diversity support. Applications include smart home devices, wireless sensors, asset tracking, health monitors, and industrial IoT. Development kits include RF evaluation boards and protocol analyzers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select BLE 5.0 for smartphone connectivity and interoperability; choose Sub-1G for long-range, low-power sensor networks.

categories.name: General Purpose MCU
categories.description: Hangshun General Purpose MCUs are 32-bit ARM Cortex-M based microcontrollers designed for a wide range of embedded applications. These MCUs offer an optimal balance of performance, power consumption, and cost. Featuring Cortex-M0, M3, and M4 cores with frequencies up to 168MHz, they provide scalable processing power for various application requirements. The portfolio includes devices with Flash memory from 32KB to 512KB and SRAM from 4KB to 128KB. Rich peripheral sets include multiple UART, SPI, I2C interfaces, USB 2.0 full-speed, CAN 2.0B, Ethernet, ADCs (12-bit to 16-bit), DACs, and advanced timers. All devices support wide voltage ranges (1.8V to 5.5V) and industrial temperature ranges (-40°C to +85°C or +105°C). These MCUs are ideal for industrial control, consumer electronics, medical devices, and general embedded systems requiring reliable processing and connectivity.

selectionGuide.title: How to Select General Purpose MCU
selectionGuide.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting the right general purpose MCU based on performance, memory, and peripheral requirements.

faqs.question: How do I select the right General Purpose MCU from Hangshun?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right General Purpose MCU involves evaluating several parameters: 1) Core performance - Choose Cortex-M0 (up to 48MHz) for simple control tasks, M3 (up to 72MHz) for medium complexity with hardware divide, or M4 (up to 168MHz) for DSP/FPU requirements; 2) Memory size - Flash from 32KB (simple applications) to 512KB (complex firmware); SRAM from 4KB to 128KB based on runtime data needs; 3) Peripherals - Count required interfaces: UART for serial communication, SPI/I2C for sensors, USB for PC connectivity, CAN for industrial networks, Ethernet for networking; 4) ADC requirements - 12-bit for general use, 16-bit for precision measurement; 5) Package - QFN for compact designs, LQFP for easy prototyping, TSSOP for cost-sensitive applications; 6) Power - Check active and sleep current for battery applications. Use the parametric search table to filter by these criteria.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use the parametric table to filter by core type, memory size, and peripherals; contact our FAEs for complex selection requirements.

faqs.question: What is the difference between HS32F103 and HS32F405 series?
faqs.answer: The HS32F103 and HS32F405 are two popular General Purpose MCU series with different target applications: HS32F103 series features Cortex-M3 core at 72MHz, 64KB-512KB Flash, 20KB-96KB SRAM, and is pin-to-pin compatible with STM32F103. It includes USB, CAN, and up to 3 ADCs. Best for industrial control, consumer electronics, and cost-sensitive applications. HS32F405 series features Cortex-M4 core at 168MHz with FPU, 512KB-1MB Flash, 128KB-192KB SRAM, and includes USB OTG, Ethernet, DCMI interface, and advanced timers. It offers significantly higher performance for DSP applications, complex algorithms, and graphics processing. HS32F103 is ideal when you need proven reliability at lower cost; HS32F405 is preferred for high-performance requirements. Both series maintain software compatibility through HAL libraries.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose HS32F103 for cost-effective industrial applications; select HS32F405 for high-performance DSP and complex processing needs.

faqs.question: How do I migrate from STM32 to Hangshun MCU?
faqs.answer: Migrating from STM32 to Hangshun MCU is straightforward due to architectural compatibility: 1) Pin compatibility - Many Hangshun MCUs are pin-to-pin compatible with STM32 (e.g., HS32F103 with STM32F103); check the pinout table for exact mapping; 2) Software compatibility - Hangshun provides HAL libraries with similar APIs to STM32 HAL; register structures are compatible for direct register access; 3) Development tools - Use the same Keil MDK, IAR, or GCC toolchains; no need to change IDE; 4) Peripheral configuration - Clock tree and peripheral initialization are similar; review clock configuration for any differences; 5) Code migration - Most application code requires minimal changes; focus on startup files and linker scripts; 6) Testing - Verify timing-critical code as there may be slight timing differences. BeiLuo provides migration guides and technical support to ensure smooth transition. Typical migration effort is 1-2 weeks for established projects.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAEs for migration support; we provide pin compatibility tables and code migration guides.

faqs.question: What packages are available for Hangshun General Purpose MCUs?
faqs.answer: Hangshun General Purpose MCUs are available in multiple package types to suit different application requirements: 1) QFN packages - QFN32 (5x5mm), QFN48 (6x6mm), QFN64 (8x8mm), QFN100 (12x12mm); smallest footprint, excellent thermal performance, requires careful PCB design; 2) LQFP packages - LQFP48 (7x7mm), LQFP64 (10x10mm), LQFP100 (14x14mm), LQFP144 (20x20mm); easy for prototyping and hand soldering, good pin accessibility; 3) TSSOP packages - TSSOP20 (6.5x4.4mm), TSSOP28, TSSOP38; cost-effective for low-pin-count applications; 4) LGA/BGA - Available for high-pin-count applications requiring smallest form factor. Package selection considerations: PCB space constraints, manufacturing capability (hand vs automated assembly), thermal requirements, and cost. QFN offers best size/performance ratio; LQFP is preferred for development and low-volume production.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose QFN for compact designs, LQFP for prototyping and easy assembly, TSSOP for cost-sensitive low-pin applications.

faqs.question: What are the power consumption characteristics of Hangshun General Purpose MCUs?
faqs.answer: Hangshun General Purpose MCUs offer competitive power consumption across all operating modes: Active mode - Cortex-M0: ~100μA/MHz, M3: ~150μA/MHz, M4: ~200μA/MHz at typical conditions (3.3V, 25°C); Sleep mode - CPU stopped, peripherals running: 50-200μA depending on enabled peripherals; Deep sleep - RAM retained, wake-up from interrupt: 5-20μA; Standby - lowest power with wake-up from reset: 1-5μA. Power optimization features: Multiple clock sources (HSI, HSE, PLL) with dynamic switching; Peripheral clock gating to disable unused modules; Voltage regulator modes (run, low-power, shutdown); Flash wait state optimization; Brown-out reset with configurable threshold. For battery-powered applications, consider: Lower operating frequency, Use sleep modes between tasks, Disable unused peripherals, Optimize clock tree configuration. Actual power consumption depends on application code, enabled peripherals, and operating voltage.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use sleep modes and clock gating for battery applications; contact our FAEs for power optimization recommendations.

products.name: HS32F103C8T6
products.shortDescription: ARM Cortex-M3 MCU, 64KB Flash, 20KB SRAM, 72MHz, LQFP48 package with rich peripherals including USB and CAN.
descriptionParagraphs: The HS32F103C8T6 is a high-performance 32-bit microcontroller based on ARM Cortex-M3 core operating at 72MHz.
It features 64KB Flash memory and 20KB SRAM, providing sufficient resources for industrial and consumer applications.
Rich peripheral set includes USB 2.0 full-speed, CAN 2.0B, 2x I2C, 3x USART, 2x SPI, and 2x 12-bit ADC.
features: 72MHz ARM Cortex-M3 core with single-cycle multiply and hardware divide
64KB Flash memory with 100,000 erase/write cycles
20KB SRAM for data and stack
USB 2.0 full-speed device/host/OTG controller
CAN 2.0B interface with baud rates up to 1Mbps
2x 12-bit ADC with 16 channels, 1μs conversion time
7-channel DMA controller for peripheral data transfer
Wide operating voltage: 2.0V to 3.6V

faeReview.author: Michael Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Embedded Systems
faeReview.content: The HS32F103C8T6 is an excellent choice for cost-sensitive applications requiring USB and CAN connectivity. In my experience supporting industrial customers, this MCU has proven to be a reliable drop-in replacement for STM32F103C8T6 with significant cost savings. The peripheral set is well-balanced for general embedded applications. I particularly recommend this part for CAN bus applications due to its robust CAN controller implementation. The 72MHz Cortex-M3 provides sufficient performance for most control tasks. For applications requiring higher pin count, consider the HS32F103RBT6 in LQFP64 package. Note that the ADC performance is good for general measurement but may require external conditioning for high-precision applications.
faeReview.highlight: Cost-effective STM32 alternative with USB and CAN support

Development board with onboard debugger and peripheral connectors

faqs.question: What is the maximum operating frequency of HS32F103C8T6?
faqs.answer: The HS32F103C8T6 operates at a maximum frequency of 72MHz using the ARM Cortex-M3 core. The clock source can be derived from: 1) Internal 8MHz RC oscillator (HSI) - factory trimmed to ±1% accuracy; 2) External 4-16MHz crystal/ceramic resonator (HSE) - required for USB operation; 3) PLL - can multiply HSI or HSE up to 72MHz. For USB applications, HSE must be 8MHz or 12MHz to generate accurate 48MHz USB clock. The core can run at lower frequencies (down to 8MHz) to reduce power consumption. Flash wait states must be configured based on operating frequency: 0 wait states up to 24MHz, 1 wait state up to 48MHz, 2 wait states up to 72MHz. The 72MHz frequency provides 1.25 DMIPS/MHz performance, suitable for real-time control and communication applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 72MHz for maximum performance; reduce frequency and wait states for power-sensitive applications.

faqs.question: How do I program and debug HS32F103C8T6?
faqs.answer: The HS32F103C8T6 supports programming and debugging through SWD (Serial Wire Debug) interface: 1) Debug interface - SWD uses 2 pins: SWDIO (PA13) and SWCLK (PA14); JTAG is also supported with 5 pins; 2) Programming tools - HS-Link (Hangshun debugger), ST-Link/V2, J-Link, or ULINK; 3) IDEs - Keil MDK-ARM, IAR Embedded Workbench, STM32CubeIDE with GCC; 4) Boot modes - System memory (factory bootloader via UART), Flash memory (user code), SRAM (debugging); 5) Programming methods - In-circuit programming via SWD, In-application programming (IAP) via UART/USB/CAN, and Bootloader mode for initial programming. For production programming, use SWD interface with a dedicated programmer. The factory bootloader supports programming via USART1 (PA9/PA10) with standard UART-to-USB converters. Flash protection features prevent unauthorized reading of programmed code.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use SWD with HS-Link or ST-Link for development; use UART bootloader for field updates.

faqs.question: What are the ADC specifications of HS32F103C8T6?
faqs.answer: The HS32F103C8T6 features two 12-bit SAR ADCs with the following specifications: Resolution - 12-bit (4096 levels); Conversion time - 1μs at 56MHz ADC clock (fastest mode); Input channels - 16 external channels (ADC1) + 2 internal channels (temperature sensor, internal reference); Input voltage range - 0V to VREF+ (typically 3.3V); Sampling rate - up to 1 Msps (million samples per second); Trigger sources - software, timer events, external interrupt; Operating modes - single conversion, continuous conversion, scan mode, discontinuous mode; DMA support - results can be transferred via DMA for high-speed acquisition; Temperature sensor - factory-calibrated for temperature monitoring; Reference voltage - internal 1.2V reference or external VREF. For best accuracy, use external VREF and add input filtering. The ADC performance is suitable for general measurement applications but may require signal conditioning for high-precision requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use single conversion for low-speed measurement; enable DMA for high-speed continuous acquisition.

faqs.question: How do I configure the USB peripheral on HS32F103C8T6?
faqs.answer: Configuring USB on HS32F103C8T6 requires several steps: 1) Clock configuration - USB requires precise 48MHz clock; use external 8MHz or 12MHz crystal with PLL to generate 48MHz; 2) GPIO configuration - USB_DM (PA11) and USB_DP (PA12) are dedicated USB pins; no remapping needed; 3) Peripheral initialization - enable USB clock in RCC, configure USB interrupt priority, initialize USB core; 4) Endpoint configuration - USB supports up to 8 bidirectional endpoints; configure endpoint type (control, bulk, interrupt, isochronous) and buffer size; 5) Firmware - use USB library (Hangshun USB Device Library or STM32 USB library); implement standard USB descriptors (device, configuration, interface, endpoint); 6) Pull-up - internal 1.5kΩ pull-up on USB_DP controlled by software for connection detection. Common USB classes supported: CDC (virtual COM port), HID (keyboard/mouse), MSC (mass storage), and custom devices. USB firmware requires approximately 8-12KB Flash depending on class implementation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use CDC class for virtual serial port; HID for simple data transfer; MSC for storage devices.

faqs.question: What are the CAN bus capabilities of HS32F103C8T6?
faqs.answer: The HS32F103C8T6 includes one CAN 2.0B interface with these capabilities: Protocol - CAN 2.0B Active (supports 11-bit and 29-bit identifiers); Baud rates - configurable from 10kbps to 1Mbps; Message objects - 3 transmit mailboxes, 2 receive FIFOs (3 messages each), 28 scalable filter banks; Operating modes - initialization, normal, sleep, loopback, silent, silent loopback (test modes); Interrupts - transmit, receive, error, status change interrupts; Timestamp - message time stamping using internal timer; Error handling - automatic retransmission, error counting, bus-off recovery. For CAN communication, external CAN transceiver (e.g., TJA1050, SN65HVD230) is required to convert logic levels to differential CAN signals. CAN bus termination requires 120Ω resistor at each end of the bus. The CAN peripheral is suitable for industrial automation, automotive diagnostics, and distributed control systems. Bit timing configuration requires careful calculation based on system clock and desired baud rate.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 125-500kbps for industrial CAN; 1Mbps for automotive; add external transceiver and termination resistors.

faqs.question: What is the Flash memory endurance and retention of HS32F103C8T6?
faqs.answer: The HS32F103C8T6 Flash memory has the following characteristics: Endurance - minimum 100,000 erase/write cycles per page; typical endurance exceeds 1,000,000 cycles; Retention - 30 years data retention at 85°C after 10,000 cycles; 10 years at 105°C; Page size - 1KB per page (64 pages total for 64KB); Erase time - 20-40ms per page; Programming time - 40-80μs per 16-bit word; Read access - 0 wait states up to 24MHz, 1 wait state up to 48MHz, 2 wait states up to 72MHz. Flash protection features: Read protection - 3 levels (none, memory read protected, full chip protected); Write protection - individual page write protection; Option bytes - store configuration data with same endurance as main Flash. For applications requiring frequent writes, implement wear leveling across multiple pages. Consider using external EEPROM or FRAM for data logging applications with very high write frequency. The Flash endurance is sufficient for firmware updates and configuration storage but may not suit high-frequency data logging.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use wear leveling for frequent writes; consider external EEPROM for data logging exceeding 100K cycles.

With 1MB Flash memory and 192KB SRAM, it supports complex applications requiring large memory and high processing power.
Advanced features include USB OTG, Ethernet MAC with DMA, camera interface, and advanced timers for motor control.
features: 168MHz ARM Cortex-M4 core with DSP instructions and single-precision FPU
1MB Flash memory with adaptive real-time accelerator (ART)
192KB SRAM including 64KB CCM (core coupled memory)

faeReview.author: David Wang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - DSP Applications
faeReview.content: The HS32F405RGT6 is Hangshun's flagship high-performance MCU, delivering exceptional value for DSP-intensive applications. I've successfully deployed this MCU in audio processing and motor control projects where the FPU significantly accelerates floating-point calculations. The 1MB Flash and 192KB SRAM provide ample resources for complex firmware including RTOS and communication stacks. The Ethernet MAC with dedicated DMA is particularly well-implemented for industrial networking applications. I recommend this part for applications requiring USB OTG host capability, as the embedded PHY reduces BOM cost. The camera interface enables simple image processing applications. Note that the high performance comes with higher power consumption, so consider sleep mode management for battery applications. Overall, this MCU competes well with STM32F407 while offering better cost-performance ratio.
faeReview.highlight: High-performance MCU with FPU, Ethernet, and USB OTG for demanding applications

faqs.question: What DSP capabilities does the Cortex-M4F in HS32F405RGT6 provide?
faqs.answer: The HS32F405RGT6's Cortex-M4F core includes powerful DSP capabilities: 1) Single-cycle MAC (Multiply-Accumulate) - 32x32=64-bit multiplication with 64-bit accumulation in one cycle; 2) SIMD instructions - Single Instruction Multiple Data operations on 8-bit and 16-bit data, enabling parallel processing of multiple samples; 3) Hardware divide - 2-12 cycle signed/unsigned division; 4) Saturating arithmetic - prevents overflow in signal processing algorithms; 5) FPU (Floating-Point Unit) - single-precision (32-bit) IEEE 754 compliant floating-point operations; 6) DSP instructions - specialized instructions for FFT, FIR filtering, and correlation. Performance metrics: 210 DMIPS at 168MHz, 3 CoreMark/MHz. DSP applications: Audio processing (EQ, filtering, compression), Motor control (FOC, PID), Digital filtering (FIR, IIR), Signal analysis (FFT). The FPU accelerates floating-point calculations 10-20x compared to software emulation. For optimal DSP performance, place critical data in CCM (Core Coupled Memory) for zero-wait-state access.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use DSP instructions for signal processing; enable FPU for floating-point algorithms; place critical data in CCM.

faqs.question: How do I implement Ethernet connectivity with HS32F405RGT6?
faqs.answer: Implementing Ethernet with HS32F405RGT6 requires both MAC and PHY configuration: 1) MAC configuration - The MCU includes IEEE 802.3-compliant Ethernet MAC with MII/RMII interface; configure MAC address, frame filtering, and DMA descriptors; 2) PHY selection - External PHY required (e.g., DP83848, LAN8720); RMII reduces pin count from 16 (MII) to 7 pins; 3) Clock requirements - 25MHz crystal for PHY; 50MHz REF_CLK from PHY to MCU (RMII mode); 4) Software stack - Use LwIP (lightweight IP) or similar TCP/IP stack; requires approximately 40KB Flash and 20KB RAM; 5) Connection - RJ45 connector with integrated magnetics or external magnetics. Key features: Dedicated DMA for zero-copy data transfer, Hardware checksum offload (IPv4, TCP, UDP), Wake-on-LAN support, Precision Time Protocol (PTP) IEEE 1588. Typical throughput: 90+ Mbps with optimized software. For industrial applications, consider external PHY with extended temperature range.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use RMII mode to save pins; select PHY with integrated magnetics for compact designs; use LwIP for TCP/IP stack.

faqs.question: What is the difference between CCM and regular SRAM in HS32F405RGT6?
faqs.answer: The HS32F405RGT6 includes two types of SRAM with different characteristics: CCM (Core Coupled Memory) - 64KB at address 0x10000000; directly connected to Cortex-M4 core bus (D-bus); zero wait-state access at full CPU speed; not accessible by DMA; ideal for critical data, stack, and frequently accessed variables; Regular SRAM - 128KB at address 0x20000000; connected to AHB bus; accessible by both CPU and DMA; suitable for general data, DMA buffers, and peripheral data; can be partitioned for different uses. Usage recommendations: Place interrupt handlers, RTOS kernel, and critical data structures in CCM; Use regular SRAM for DMA buffers, large data arrays, and peripheral communication buffers; Stack can be placed in CCM for fastest context switching. Note that CCM is not accessible by DMA, so data requiring DMA transfer must be in regular SRAM. The linker script must be configured to place sections appropriately. Performance benefit: CCM provides 20-30% faster access for critical code compared to regular SRAM.
faqs.decisionGuide: Place critical code and data in CCM; use regular SRAM for DMA buffers and large data structures.

faqs.question: How does the USB OTG feature work on HS32F405RGT6?
faqs.answer: The HS32F405RGT6 supports USB OTG (On-The-Go) with both device and host capabilities: 1) USB OTG FS (Full-Speed) - 12Mbps, embedded PHY, supports device and host modes; 2) USB OTG HS (High-Speed) - 480Mbps, requires external ULPI PHY; 3) Device mode - operates as USB peripheral (CDC, HID, MSC classes); 4) Host mode - can control USB devices (mass storage, HID devices); 5) OTG mode - dynamically switches between host and device based on cable connection; 6) VBUS management - internal charge pump for VBUS power in host mode. Key features: 8 bidirectional endpoints, 4KB dedicated packet buffer memory, dynamic FIFO allocation, Session Request Protocol (SRP) and Host Negotiation Protocol (HNP) for OTG. Typical applications: Device mode - virtual COM port, firmware upgrade interface; Host mode - USB flash drive access, connecting USB peripherals; OTG mode - peer-to-peer communication between devices. The embedded PHY in full-speed mode reduces BOM cost compared to external PHY solutions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use OTG FS with embedded PHY for most applications; add external ULPI PHY for high-speed requirements.

faqs.question: What camera interface capabilities does HS32F405RGT6 support?
faqs.answer: The HS32F405RGT6 includes a DCMI (Digital Camera Interface) for connecting image sensors: Interface type - parallel 8/10/12/14-bit data bus; Timing signals - PIXCLK (pixel clock), HSYNC (horizontal sync), VSYNC (vertical sync); Data capture - supports continuous and snapshot modes; Data formats - RAW8, RAW10, RAW12, YUV422, RGB565, JPEG compression; FIFO - 32-bit wide, 4-word deep FIFO for data buffering; DMA - dedicated DMA channel for memory-to-memory transfer; Synchronization - programmable polarity for all timing signals; Crop feature - programmable cropping window to reduce image size. Compatible sensors: OV7670, OV2640, OV7725, and other parallel interface sensors. Maximum throughput depends on pixel clock frequency (typically up to 54MHz). Applications: Simple image capture, barcode/QR code scanning, visual inspection systems, camera-based user interfaces. Note that the DCMI captures raw data; image processing (compression, encoding) must be handled by software or external components.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use DCMI for simple image capture; consider external image processor for complex image processing requirements.

faqs.question: How do I optimize power consumption on HS32F405RGT6?
faqs.answer: Optimizing power consumption on HS32F405RGT6 involves multiple strategies: 1) Clock management - Use lowest frequency meeting performance requirements; disable unused peripheral clocks; use HSI instead of HSE when precision is not critical; 2) Sleep modes - Sleep (CPU stopped, peripherals running): 0.5mA typical; Stop (regulators in low-power, RAM retained): 0.1mA; Standby (only backup domain active): 2.5μA; 3) Peripheral optimization - Disable unused peripherals; reduce peripheral clock frequencies; use batch processing to minimize active time; 4) Voltage scaling - Three voltage ranges: Range 1 (1.8V, max performance), Range 2 (1.5V, balanced), Range 3 (1.2V, low power); 5) Flash wait states - Optimize for operating frequency to reduce power; 6) GPIO configuration - Configure unused pins as analog input to minimize leakage. Power consumption examples: Active (168MHz, all peripherals): 60mA; Sleep (168MHz): 25mA; Stop with RTC: 120μA; Standby: 2.5μA. For battery applications, use Stop mode between tasks with RTC for periodic wake-up.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use voltage scaling and sleep modes for battery applications; disable unused peripherals and optimize clock frequencies.

categories.name: Touch Control MCU
categories.description: Hangshun Touch Control MCUs integrate advanced capacitive touch sensing technology with powerful 32-bit ARM Cortex-M cores. These specialized MCUs support up to 24 capacitive touch channels with high sensitivity, enabling touch buttons, sliders, wheels, and proximity sensing through various overlay materials including glass, plastic, and acrylic up to 5mm thick. The integrated touch controller features water rejection technology for reliable operation in wet environments, making them ideal for kitchen appliances and outdoor applications. With ultra-low power consumption of less than 10μA per channel in standby mode and wake-on-touch capability, these MCUs are perfect for battery-powered devices. Advanced touch algorithms provide fast response time under 10ms and excellent noise immunity with ESD protection up to ±8kV contact discharge. The touch library simplifies development with automatic calibration, gesture recognition, and waterproofing algorithms. Applications include home appliance control panels, industrial HMI interfaces, consumer electronics, medical devices, and automotive interiors.

faqs.question: How many touch channels do I need for my application?
faqs.answer: The number of touch channels required depends on your user interface design: 1) Simple buttons - Each physical button requires one touch channel; typical home appliance panels need 4-12 buttons (power, mode, timer, +/-, etc.); 2) Sliders - Linear sliders typically use 3-5 channels for smooth position detection; circular sliders (wheels) use 4-8 channels; 3) Keypads - Matrix keypads can share channels with time-multiplexing; a 3x4 keypad can use 7 channels (3 rows + 4 columns) instead of 12; 4) Proximity sensing - Requires 1-2 channels for wake-on-approach functionality; 5) Reserved channels - Add 10-20% extra channels for future expansion. Common configurations: Simple appliance (4-6 buttons): 6-8 channels; Washing machine panel (8-12 buttons + slider): 12-16 channels; Advanced HMI (buttons + slider + wheel): 16-24 channels. Hangshun touch MCUs offer 6, 12, 16, and 24 channel variants. It's better to select a part with slightly more channels than needed to accommodate design changes.
faqs.decisionGuide: Count your buttons and sliders; add 20% margin; select nearest channel count variant (6/12/16/24 channels).

faqs.question: What overlay materials work with Hangshun touch MCUs?
faqs.answer: Hangshun touch MCUs support various overlay materials with different thickness capabilities: 1) Plastic/Acrylic - Best sensitivity; supports up to 5mm thickness; commonly used for appliance panels; lowest cost option; 2) Glass - Premium appearance; supports up to 4mm thickness; requires higher sensitivity setting; tempered glass recommended for durability; 3) Ceramic - High-end aesthetic; supports up to 3mm thickness; used in premium kitchen appliances; 4) Wood - Natural appearance; supports up to 2mm thickness; requires careful design due to moisture absorption; 5) Metal - Only for proximity sensing (not direct touch); metal must have cutouts behind touch areas. Material properties affecting performance: Dielectric constant (higher = better coupling); Thickness (thicker = lower signal); Uniformity (variations cause sensitivity differences); Moisture absorption (affects stability). For best results: Keep overlay thickness uniform; Minimize air gaps between sensor and overlay; Use adhesive with consistent thickness; Design ground plane to reduce noise pickup. Water rejection algorithms enable reliable operation even with water droplets on the surface.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use plastic/acrylic for cost-sensitive; glass for premium; ensure uniform thickness and proper bonding.

faqs.question: How does water rejection technology work on Hangshun touch MCUs?
faqs.answer: Hangshun touch MCUs implement advanced water rejection technology for reliable operation in wet environments: 1) Problem - Water on touch surface creates capacitive coupling similar to finger touch, causing false triggers; 2) Detection method - Hardware detects water presence by measuring capacitance distribution across multiple channels; water creates different capacitance pattern than finger; 3) Rejection algorithm - Software algorithms distinguish between water film and valid touch; water typically affects multiple adjacent channels simultaneously; finger touch has localized signature; 4) Shielding - Guard ring around touch pads reduces water interference; driven shield maintains constant voltage to reduce water coupling; 5) Dynamic threshold - Touch threshold automatically adjusts based on environmental conditions. Water handling capabilities: Light spray/mist - Full rejection, normal operation; Water droplets - Rejection with continued valid touch detection; Water stream - May cause temporary disablement until water clears; Submersion - Not recommended (IP rating dependent on enclosure). Applications benefiting from water rejection: Washing machines, dishwashers, kitchen appliances, outdoor panels, bathroom controls. The water rejection feature is configurable via touch library parameters.
faqs.decisionGuide: Enable water rejection for kitchen/outdoor applications; configure sensitivity based on expected water exposure level.

faqs.question: What is the power consumption of touch sensing in standby mode?
faqs.answer: Hangshun touch MCUs offer ultra-low power consumption for battery-powered applications: Active scanning mode - 50-100μA per channel at 10Hz scan rate; depends on sensitivity setting and overlay thickness; Sleep with wake-on-touch - 5-10μA total for all channels; MCU in deep sleep, touch controller in low-power mode; Wake-on-touch detection - <100ms response time from sleep to active; Proximity sensing - 20-30μA for 1-2 proximity channels; Periodic scanning - Adjustable scan interval from 10ms to 1s; longer intervals reduce power. Power optimization techniques: Reduce scan frequency in standby (e.g., 5Hz instead of 50Hz); Use proximity wake-up to enable full touch only when hand approaches; Disable unused channels; Optimize sensitivity (higher sensitivity = higher power); Use sleep mode between scans. Example power budgets: 6-button remote control (wake-on-touch): <10μA standby; 12-channel appliance panel (active): 500μA; 24-channel HMI with proximity: 30μA standby. For battery applications, the touch MCU can operate for years on coin cell batteries with proper power management.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use wake-on-touch for battery applications; reduce scan frequency in standby; enable proximity wake-up.

faqs.question: How do I design touch sensor PCBs for Hangshun touch MCUs?
faqs.answer: PCB design for capacitive touch sensors requires attention to several factors: 1) Sensor shapes - Circular pads for buttons (8-12mm diameter); Interlocking triangles for sliders; Concentric circles for wheels; 2) Trace routing - Keep traces short (<100mm) to reduce parasitic capacitance; Route traces on opposite side from touch pads; Use 0.2-0.3mm trace width; Maintain 0.5mm spacing between traces; 3) Ground plane - Solid ground plane on layer below touch pads; Keep 0.5-1mm gap between sensor and ground; Use hatched ground (not solid) directly under sensors for better sensitivity; 4) Shielding - Add guard ring around sensors, driven at same potential; Reduces noise and improves water rejection; 5) Components - Place MCU close to sensor array; Minimize vias in touch traces; Use low-ESR bypass capacitors near MCU. Material considerations: Standard FR-4 works well; Thinner PCBs (0.8-1.0mm) provide better sensitivity; Keep overlay material consistent. Testing recommendations: Verify sensitivity with actual overlay material; Test with water droplets if water rejection required; Check for false triggers in noisy environments.
faqs.decisionGuide: Keep traces short; use hatched ground under sensors; add guard ring; test with actual overlay material.

faqs.question: How do I calibrate touch sensitivity on HS32T016C8T6?
faqs.answer: Touch sensitivity calibration on HS32T016C8T6 involves hardware and software configuration: 1) Sensitivity levels - Each channel has 256 sensitivity levels (0-255); higher values = higher sensitivity; 2) Auto-calibration - The touch library includes auto-calibration at power-up; compensates for PCB variations and overlay capacitance; runs for approximately 500ms at startup; 3) Manual tuning - Use the tuning tool to find optimal sensitivity; target signal-to-noise ratio > 10:1; adjust until reliable detection with no false triggers; 4) Environmental compensation - Auto-calibration periodically updates baseline; handles temperature drift and humidity changes; 5) Threshold settings - Touch threshold (typically 80% of signal); Release threshold (typically 60% of signal); Hysteresis prevents oscillation. Calibration procedure: Install actual overlay material; Power on and let auto-calibration complete; Test all touch areas for reliable detection; Adjust sensitivity if needed (increase if missed touches, decrease if false triggers); Save calibration values to Flash. The water rejection feature has separate sensitivity settings for wet conditions.

faqs.question: What is the response time of HS32T016C8T6 touch detection?
faqs.answer: The HS32T016C8T6 offers fast touch response times for responsive user interfaces: Touch detection latency - <10ms from physical touch to detection flag; includes ADC conversion and algorithm processing; Scan rate - Configurable from 10Hz to 100Hz; higher rates reduce latency but increase power; typical setting: 50Hz (20ms scan period); Wake-on-touch - <100ms from deep sleep to active mode; includes oscillator startup and initialization; Report rate - Touch coordinate updates at scan rate; 50Hz scan = 50 reports/second; Slider/wheel tracking - Smooth interpolation between scans; no perceived lag during continuous movement; Multi-touch - Sequential channel scanning; simultaneous touches detected within same scan cycle. Factors affecting response time: Number of enabled channels (more channels = longer scan); Sensitivity setting (higher sensitivity = longer charge transfer); Overlay thickness (thicker = longer charge time); Scan frequency setting. For gaming or fast-input applications, use 100Hz scan rate. For battery-powered devices, 10-20Hz provides good response with low power.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 50-100Hz for responsive UI; 10-20Hz for battery-powered; wake-on-touch <100ms from sleep.

faqs.question: How does the ESD protection work on HS32T016C8T6 touch channels?
faqs.answer: The HS32T016C8T6 includes robust ESD protection on all touch channels: Protection level - ±8kV contact discharge (IEC 61000-4-2 Level 4); ±15kV air discharge; Protection circuitry - Integrated ESD protection diodes on each touch pin; Series resistors (typically 1kΩ) recommended in series with each channel; Clamping diodes to VDD and VSS; Design recommendations - Add 1kΩ series resistors close to touch pads; Keep ESD discharge path away from sensitive circuitry; Use ground plane for ESD current return; Consider TVS diodes for extreme environments; Certification - IEC 61000-4-2 Level 4 compliant; Suitable for consumer and industrial applications. ESD events can cause: Temporary touch lock-up (recovers automatically); False touch detection (filtered by software); In extreme cases, MCU reset (watchdog protection). Recovery features: Automatic baseline reset after ESD event; Software filtering to ignore ESD-induced transients; Watchdog timer for system recovery. For medical or automotive applications, additional external protection may be required. The ESD protection is tested with standard ESD gun per IEC 61000-4-2.
faqs.decisionGuide: Add 1kΩ series resistors for additional protection; ensure proper grounding; consider TVS for harsh environments.

faqs.question: Can HS32T016C8T6 work with gloves or thick overlays?
faqs.answer: The HS32T016C8T6 can be configured for glove operation and thick overlays with proper settings: Glove detection - Enable high sensitivity mode; detects touch through thin gloves (latex, fabric); thick work gloves may not be detectable; response time may increase slightly in glove mode; Thick overlays - Supports up to 5mm glass or plastic; sensitivity must be increased for thick overlays; may require slower scan rate for reliable detection; trade-off between sensitivity and noise immunity; Configuration options: Sensitivity level - Increase to 200-255 for glove/thick overlay; Charge time - Increase charge transfer time; Scan frequency - Reduce to 20-30Hz for better signal integration; Noise filtering - Enable additional digital filtering; Limitations: Very thick overlays (>5mm) may not work reliably; Thick rubber gloves may not be detected; Metal-containing gloves interfere with capacitive sensing; Wet gloves may cause false detection. categories.name: Motor Control MCU
categories.description: Hangshun Motor Control MCUs are specialized microcontrollers designed for efficient control of various motor types including BLDC (Brushless DC), PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor), stepper, and brushed DC motors. These MCUs integrate advanced motor control peripherals including high-resolution PWM units with dead-time insertion, integrated gate drivers for external MOSFETs, analog comparators for overcurrent protection, and operational amplifiers for current sensing. The ARM Cortex-M4 core with FPU enables complex control algorithms such as Field Oriented Control (FOC) and Sensorless Vector Control. Hardware acceleration for trigonometric functions and PID controllers reduces CPU load. The MCUs support both sensored (Hall effect, encoder) and sensorless (BEMF, observer) control methods. Integrated protection features include overcurrent, overvoltage, undervoltage, and overtemperature protection. With PWM frequencies up to 100kHz and dead-time resolution down to 1ns, these MCUs provide precise motor control for applications ranging from small fans to high-power industrial drives. Power range extends from 10W to 2kW depending on external power stage selection.

selectionGuide.title: Motor Control MCU Selection Guide
selectionGuide.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting motor control MCU based on motor type, power level, and control algorithm requirements.

faqs.question: How do I select the right motor control MCU for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right motor control MCU depends on motor type, power level, and control requirements: 1) Motor type - BLDC (6-step or FOC): Need 6 PWM channels, commutation logic; PMSM (FOC): Need 6 PWM channels, high-performance CPU for Clarke/Park transforms; Stepper: Need 2-4 PWM channels, microstepping support; Brushed DC: Need 1-2 PWM channels, simple control; 2) Power level - Low power (<100W): Integrated drivers may be sufficient; Medium power (100W-1kW): External MOSFETs with gate drivers; High power (>1kW): External IGBTs with isolated drivers; 3) Control algorithm - 6-step commutation: Basic MCU with simple peripherals; FOC/Sensorless: Cortex-M4 with FPU recommended for real-time calculations; 4) Sensing requirements - Current sensing: Integrated op-amps reduce external components; Position sensing: Encoder/Hall interfaces for sensored control; Sensorless: High-speed ADC for BEMF detection; 5) Safety features: Overcurrent protection, fault inputs, emergency stop. Use the parametric table to filter by PWM channels, CPU performance, and integrated features.
faqs.decisionGuide: Match PWM channels to motor type; select M4+FPU for FOC; check integrated op-amps for current sensing.

faqs.question: What is the difference between 6-step and FOC motor control?
faqs.answer: Two main control methods for BLDC/PMSM motors have different characteristics: 6-Step (Trapezoidal) Control - Principle: Energize 2 of 3 phases at a time (6 combinations); Complexity: Simple, low computational requirements; Efficiency: Good (85-90%); Torque ripple: Higher (10-15%); Speed range: Limited (typically 1:10); Sensor requirements: Hall sensors or sensorless BEMF; CPU load: Low (any Cortex-M0/M3); Best for: Cost-sensitive applications, high-speed motors. FOC (Field Oriented Control) - Principle: Control torque and flux independently like DC motor; Complexity: Complex, requires coordinate transforms (Clarke/Park); Efficiency: Excellent (90-95%); Torque ripple: Very low (<5%); Speed range: Wide (1:100 or more); Sensor requirements: Encoder preferred, sensorless possible; CPU load: High (Cortex-M4 with FPU recommended); Best for: High-performance servo, energy-efficient applications. Selection guidance: Use 6-step for fans, pumps, simple drives where cost matters; Use FOC for servo systems, high-efficiency drives, low-speed torque control. Hangshun motor control MCUs support both methods with appropriate libraries.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 6-step for cost-sensitive fans/pumps; use FOC for servo and high-efficiency applications.

faqs.question: What motor control libraries does Hangshun provide?
faqs.answer: Hangshun provides comprehensive motor control firmware libraries: 1) 6-Step BLDC Library - Sensorless BEMF detection; Hall sensor-based commutation; Speed and current control loops; Starting algorithms (alignment, ramp-up); Fault protection; 2) FOC Library - Complete FOC implementation (Clarke, Park, SVPWM); Sensorless observer (sliding mode, flux observer); Encoder-based position control; MTPA (Maximum Torque Per Ampere) optimization; Field weakening for high-speed operation; 3) Stepper Library - Full-step, half-step, microstepping (up to 256x); Acceleration/deceleration profiles; Stall detection; Closed-loop control with encoder; 4) PMSM Library - Optimized for servo applications; Position, speed, torque control modes; Auto-tuning for motor parameters. Library features: Modular architecture for easy integration; Configurable parameters via header files; HAL-based for portability; Example projects for common motor types; Real-time debugging support. Resource requirements: 6-step: 8-12KB Flash, 2KB RAM; FOC: 20-30KB Flash, 4KB RAM; Stepper: 6-10KB Flash, 1KB RAM. The libraries are provided as source code with full documentation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 6-step library for simple BLDC; FOC library for high-performance; stepper library for precise positioning.

faqs.question: How do I implement current sensing in motor control applications?
faqs.answer: Current sensing is critical for motor control and can be implemented several ways with Hangshun MCUs: 1) Shunt resistor sensing - Place low-value resistor (0.01-0.1Ω) in current path; Measure voltage drop with integrated op-amp; Advantages: Low cost, accurate, bidirectional; Disadvantages: Power loss, requires amplification; 2) Integrated op-amp usage - Hangshun motor MCUs include 2-3 op-amps; Configure for differential or single-ended mode; Programmable gain (typically 1-16x); Direct connection to ADC; 3) Sensing configurations - Single shunt: One resistor on DC bus, reconstruct phase currents; Dual shunt: Two resistors on lower phases; Triple shunt: Three resistors for direct measurement; 4) Isolated sensing - Hall effect sensors for high-voltage applications; Current transformers for AC measurement; Isolated amplifiers for safety-critical systems. Design considerations: Shunt power rating: P = I² × R; Op-amp bandwidth > switching frequency; ADC sampling synchronized with PWM; Offset calibration for accuracy. Typical implementation: 0.05Ω shunt resistor; Op-amp gain of 10x; 12-bit ADC resolution; Provides current resolution of ~50mA. The integrated op-amps reduce external component count significantly.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use shunt resistors with integrated op-amps for cost-effective sensing; consider Hall sensors for high voltage isolation.

faqs.question: What protection features are important for motor control MCUs?
faqs.answer: Motor control applications require robust protection features: 1) Overcurrent protection (OCP) - Fast analog comparator detection (<1μs); Shutdown PWM immediately on fault; Configurable threshold via DAC or external reference; Essential for power stage protection; 2) Overvoltage protection (OVP) - Detects regenerative braking overvoltage; Triggers braking resistor or shutdown; Protects DC bus capacitors; 3) Undervoltage protection (UVP) - Prevents operation with insufficient gate drive; Configurable threshold (typically 80% of nominal); Safe shutdown sequence; 4) Overtemperature protection - Internal MCU temperature sensor; External NTC for power stage monitoring; Thermal derating or shutdown; 5) Additional protections - Stall detection (motor stuck); Open-phase detection (wiring fault); Over-speed protection; Watchdog for software faults. Implementation: Hardware comparators for fast OCP (<1μs); Software monitoring for other protections (10-100ms); Safe state configuration on fault; Fault history logging for diagnostics. Hangshun motor control MCUs integrate most protection features, reducing external component count. The fast OCP is critical - software-only protection is too slow for power stage protection.
faqs.decisionGuide: Implement hardware OCP for power stage protection; add software monitoring for other faults; configure safe states.

faqs.question: What PWM frequency should I use for motor control?
faqs.answer: PWM frequency selection for motor control involves trade-offs between audible noise, switching losses, and control precision: 1) Audible noise - Frequencies below 16kHz produce audible whine; Use >20kHz for silent operation; 16-20kHz may be acceptable for industrial environments; 2) Switching losses - Higher frequency = higher switching losses; Losses proportional to frequency; Thermal management becomes critical >50kHz; 3) Control precision - Higher frequency = better current control; Current ripple decreases with higher frequency; Torque ripple reduced at higher frequencies; 4) Motor type considerations - BLDC fans: 16-20kHz (balance noise/losses); High-speed motors: 40-100kHz (better control); Servo motors: 20-40kHz (precision control); Power tools: 16-25kHz (audible acceptable). Typical frequencies: Low-power BLDC: 20kHz; Industrial drives: 8-16kHz; High-performance servo: 40-100kHz. The HS32M100C8T6 supports up to 100kHz PWM. Consider using variable frequency - lower at low speed, higher at high speed. Dead-time must be recalculated when changing frequency.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 20kHz for silent operation; 16kHz acceptable for industrial; increase for high-speed motors.

faqs.question: How do I calculate dead-time for motor control PWM?
faqs.answer: Dead-time prevents shoot-through in half-bridge configurations: 1) Shoot-through - Both high-side and low-side MOSFETs conducting simultaneously; Causes short circuit of DC bus; Results in excessive current and device failure; 2) Dead-time calculation - Minimum dead-time = MOSFET turn-off delay - turn-on delay + safety margin; Typical formula: Tdead = Td(off) - Td(on) + 2×Tmargin; 3) Timing parameters - MOSFET turn-on delay (Td(on)): 10-50ns; MOSFET turn-off delay (Td(off)): 30-100ns; Gate driver propagation delay: 20-50ns; Safety margin: 100-200ns; 4) Example calculation - Td(off) = 80ns, Td(on) = 30ns, Driver delay = 30ns, Margin = 150ns; Tdead = 80 - 30 + 2×30 + 150 = 260ns; Round up to 300ns for safety. Effects of dead-time: Too short: Risk of shoot-through; Too long: Distortion at current zero-crossing; Reduced effective duty cycle. The HS32M100C8T6 provides 1ns dead-time resolution for precise control. Start with conservative value (500ns-1μs) and reduce while monitoring for shoot-through.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate based on MOSFET timing; add 100-200ns margin; start conservative and optimize.

faqs.question: How do I implement sensorless BLDC control with HS32M100C8T6?
faqs.answer: Sensorless BLDC control uses BEMF (Back EMF) detection instead of Hall sensors: 1) Principle - BEMF is proportional to motor speed; Zero-crossing detection indicates commutation point; Only 2 phases energized at a time in 6-step control; Third phase provides BEMF signal; 2) Zero-crossing detection - Sample BEMF of unenergized phase; Compare to virtual neutral point (or DC bus/2); Detect when BEMF crosses zero; 3) Commutation timing - Delay 30 electrical degrees after zero-crossing; Then commutate to next step; Timing critical for smooth operation; 4) Starting method - Alignment: Energize one phase to align rotor; Ramp-up: Gradually increase speed while detecting BEMF; Open-loop to closed-loop transition; 5) Implementation with HS32M100C8T6 - Use ADC to sample BEMF voltage; Comparator for fast zero-crossing detection; PWM synchronization for sampling timing; Hardware support for virtual neutral generation. Challenges: Low BEMF at startup requires open-loop starting; Speed estimation accuracy affects performance; Load changes can cause loss of sync. The Hangshun 6-step library includes complete sensorless implementation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Hangshun sensorless library; ensure proper ADC sampling timing; tune startup ramp for your motor.

faqs.question: What is the maximum motor power I can control with HS32M100C8T6?
faqs.answer: The maximum motor power depends on external power stage and cooling: MCU limitations - PWM frequency: Up to 100kHz; Gate drive current: 200mA (can drive gates directly for small MOSFETs); ADC sampling: 2Msps for current control; Protection response: <1μs for overcurrent. Power stage considerations: Voltage: Limited by MOSFET/IGBT rating (typically 24V-600V); Current: Limited by MOSFET rating and thermal management; Switching: Gate drive capability affects switching speed; Cooling: Heatsink requirements increase with power. Typical power ranges: Direct gate drive (no external drivers): Up to 100W; With external gate drivers: Up to 1kW; With isolated drivers and IGBTs: Up to 2kW+; Practical examples: 24V BLDC fan: 50W; 48V E-bike: 500W; 310V BLDC (1-phase AC): 750W; 540V servo drive: 1.5kW. The HS32M100C8T6 is suitable for most applications up to 1kW. For higher power, ensure: Adequate gate drive for large MOSFETs; Proper current sensing and protection; Sufficient thermal management; Isolated gate drivers for high voltage. The MCU itself can control any power level with appropriate external power stage.
faqs.decisionGuide: Up to 100W with direct drive; up to 1kW with external drivers; select MOSFETs based on voltage/current requirements.

faqs.question: How do I tune the current control loop for motor control?
faqs.answer: Current loop tuning is critical for stable motor control: 1) Current loop purpose - Controls motor torque (torque proportional to current); Inner loop of cascaded control (current -> speed -> position); Fast response required (bandwidth typically 1-5kHz); 2) PI controller structure - Proportional gain (Kp): Responds to immediate error; Integral gain (Ki): Eliminates steady-state error; Anti-windup: Prevents integral saturation; 3) Tuning procedure - Start with Ki = 0, increase Kp until oscillation; Back off Kp to 50-70% of oscillation value; Add Ki to eliminate steady-state error; Typical ratio: Ki = Kp × (switching frequency / 10); 4) Verification - Step response: Rise time < 1ms, no overshoot; Stability: No oscillation under load transients; Bandwidth: Target 1-5kHz depending on application. Practical tips: Use current sampling synchronized with PWM center; Filter current feedback (100-500Hz low-pass); Limit current reference to safe values; Implement feedforward for better response. The Hangshun motor libraries include auto-tuning functions. For manual tuning, use oscilloscope to monitor current response to step changes.
faqs.decisionGuide: Tune Kp first for response, then Ki for accuracy; verify with step response; target 1-5kHz bandwidth.

faqs.question: What encoder interfaces does HS32M100C8T6 support?
faqs.answer: The HS32M100C8T6 supports multiple encoder interfaces for position feedback: 1) Quadrature encoder (A/B/Z) - Two phase-shifted signals (A and B) for direction and position; Index pulse (Z) for absolute position reference; 4x decoding (count on both edges of both signals); Interface: Connect A, B, Z to timer inputs; Hardware quadrature decoder in timer peripheral; Maximum frequency: Typically 1-5MHz depending on timer; 2) Hall sensor interface - 3 digital inputs for 60° or 120° Hall sensors; Used for commutation in BLDC motors; Can be used for coarse position feedback; Interface: Connect to GPIO or capture inputs; 3) SSI (Synchronous Serial Interface) - Absolute encoder interface; Clock and data signals; Provides absolute position on power-up; Interface: SPI or custom GPIO bit-bang. Configuration: Timer setup for quadrature mode; Pull-up resistors on encoder lines (typically 4.7kΩ); Noise filtering if needed; Z-pulse interrupt for homing. Resolution calculation: Encoder lines × 4 (quadrature) × gear ratio = counts/revolution. Example: 1000 line encoder with 10:1 gear = 40,000 counts/rev. The hardware quadrature decoder reduces CPU load significantly compared to software decoding.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use quadrature for incremental encoders; SSI for absolute encoders; Hall for BLDC commutation.

faqs.question: What advantages does FPU provide for FOC motor control?
faqs.answer: The Floating-Point Unit (FPU) in HS32M200RGT6 provides significant advantages for FOC: 1) Coordinate transforms - Park transform: 3 sine/cosine calculations, 4 multiplies; Clarke transform: 2 multiplies; FPU computes in single cycle vs 10-20 cycles in software; 2) PID controllers - Floating-point PID eliminates scaling issues; Direct implementation of continuous-time equations; No quantization errors from fixed-point; 3) Trigonometric functions - SIN/COS for Park transform; ATAN2 for position calculation; FPU single-cycle vs library function (100+ cycles); 4) Matrix operations - State observers use matrix math; FPU accelerates vector/matrix operations; Performance improvement: 10-20x faster matrix math. Cycle count comparison (typical FOC loop): Fixed-point: 500-1000 cycles; FPU: 100-200 cycles; At 168MHz: Fixed-point: 3-6μs; FPU: 0.6-1.2μs; Benefits: Higher PWM frequency possible; More complex algorithms (MTPA, field weakening); Dual-axis control from single MCU; Lower CPU load leaves time for communication/diagnostics. The FPU is essential for high-performance servo applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: FPU essential for high-performance FOC; enables complex algorithms and higher PWM frequencies.

faqs.question: How do I implement field weakening for high-speed operation?
faqs.answer: Field weakening extends motor speed beyond base speed: 1) Principle - At base speed: Back EMF = DC bus voltage; Cannot increase speed without losing current control; Field weakening: Reduce flux (field) to reduce back EMF; Allows higher speed at reduced torque; 2) Implementation - Standard FOC controls Id (flux) and Iq (torque); Below base speed: Id = 0, Iq = torque command; Above base speed: Id < 0 (negative, weakens field), Iq limited by voltage; 3) Control strategy - Constant torque region: Id=0, max Iq; Constant power region: Negative Id, reduced Iq; Limited by current rating and thermal limits; 4) HS32M200RGT6 implementation - Advanced FOC library includes field weakening; Automatic transition based on speed/voltage; MTPA (Maximum Torque Per Ampere) at low speed; Field weakening at high speed. Trade-offs: Reduced torque at high speed (constant power); Increased copper losses from Id current; Motor heating increases; Demagnetization risk for permanent magnets. Typical speed range extension: 1.5x to 3x base speed; Depends on motor design and current capacity. The field weakening algorithm requires accurate motor parameters (Ld, Lq, flux linkage).
faqs.decisionGuide: Use field weakening for 1.5-3x speed extension; monitor motor heating; implement demagnetization protection.

faqs.question: What is MTPA and how does it improve efficiency?
faqs.answer: MTPA (Maximum Torque Per Ampere) optimizes motor efficiency: 1) Problem - In standard FOC, Id=0 control is simple but not optimal; Reluctance torque not utilized in IPMSM; Higher current required for same torque; 2) MTPA principle - For IPMSM (Interior Permanent Magnet Syn. Motor): Torque = P×Flux×Iq + P×(Ld-Lq)×Id×Iq; First term: Magnet torque; Second term: Reluctance torque; MTPA finds Id, Iq combination for minimum current at given torque; 3) Implementation - Calculate optimal Id based on Iq command; Id = f(Iq, motor parameters); Requires knowledge of Ld, Lq, flux linkage; Look-up table or real-time calculation; 4) Benefits - 10-20% reduction in current for same torque; Lower copper losses (I²R); Improved efficiency, especially at high torque; Reduced heating. Trade-offs: More complex control; Parameter sensitivity; Lookup table requires calibration. The HS32M200RGT6 advanced FOC library includes MTPA with auto-calibration. Effectiveness: Most beneficial for IPMSM; Less benefit for SPMSM (surface mount); Significant for high-torque, continuous operation applications. Combined with field weakening, provides optimal efficiency across entire speed range.
faqs.decisionGuide: Enable MTPA for IPMSM applications; use auto-calibration; monitor actual efficiency improvement.

faqs.question: How do I implement dual-axis control with HS32M200RGT6?
faqs.answer: The HS32M200RGT6 supports dual-axis motor control from a single MCU: 1) Resource allocation - PWM: 4 channels per axis (8 total); ADC: 2-3 channels per axis (6 total available); Timers: 1 per axis for control loop; CPU: FOC algorithm for both axes; 2) Control architecture - Independent control loops for each axis; Synchronized PWM for multi-axis systems; Shared DC bus consideration; 3) Implementation - Axis 1: PWM1-4, ADC1-2, Timer1; Axis 2: PWM5-8, ADC3-4, Timer2; Current sampling synchronized with respective PWM; Control loops executed sequentially or interleaved; 4) Timing considerations - Total FOC time: 1-2μs per axis at 168MHz; PWM period: 50μs (20kHz); CPU load: ~10% for dual axis; Plenty of time for communication and diagnostics. Applications: CNC machines (X/Y axis); Robot joints (multiple axes); Dual spindle drives; Coordinated motion systems. Synchronization: Use same PWM time base for synchronized axes; Phase shift PWM to reduce DC bus ripple; Interleaved ADC sampling to avoid conflicts. The 168MHz FPU provides sufficient performance for dual high-performance servo axes.
faqs.decisionGuide: Allocate 4 PWM + 3 ADC per axis; use synchronized PWM; verify CPU load with both axes running.

faqs.question: What is a sensorless observer and how does it work?
faqs.answer: A sensorless observer estimates motor position without mechanical sensors: 1) Problem - Encoders add cost, wiring, reliability concerns; Hall sensors provide coarse position only; Sensorless eliminates mechanical sensors; 2) Observer principle - Mathematical model of motor in software; Inputs: Voltage commands, measured currents; Outputs: Estimated position and speed; Corrects estimate based on current error; 3) Common observer types - Luenberger observer: Linear state observer; Sliding mode observer: Robust to parameter variations; Flux observer: Estimates rotor flux position; Extended Kalman Filter: Optimal estimation; 4) Implementation - Requires accurate motor parameters (R, L, flux); High-performance CPU for real-time calculation; FPU essential for complex observers; Convergence time: 10-100ms depending on algorithm. Advantages: Lower system cost; Reduced wiring; Higher reliability; Works at most speeds. Limitations: Poor performance at very low speed (<5% rated); Requires accurate motor parameters; Complex tuning; May lose position on sudden load changes. The HS32M200RGT6 advanced FOC library includes sliding mode observer with auto-tuning. Best for: Applications where low-speed performance not critical; Cost-sensitive servo systems; High-speed spindles.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use sensorless for cost reduction and reliability; ensure accurate motor parameters; verify low-speed performance.

faqs.question: How do I implement safe torque off (STO) with HS32M200RGT6?
faqs.answer: Safe Torque Off (STO) is a safety function for emergency stop: 1) STO principle - Safe removal of torque-producing energy; Motor coasts to stop or mechanical brake applied; Prevents unexpected restart; SIL 3 / PLe safety level; 2) Implementation - Hardware: Redundant PWM disable inputs; Independent of MCU software; Force gate drivers to inactive state; Software: Monitor STO input status; Enter safe state when STO active; Prevent restart until safe condition confirmed; 3) HS32M200RGT6 features - Dedicated fault input pins; Hardware PWM disable independent of CPU; Configurable safe state (high-Z or low-side on); Fault status registers for diagnostics; 4) System design - Dual-channel STO inputs (redundancy); External safety relay or safety PLC; Verification of PWM disable on both channels; Periodic safety function testing. Safety considerations: STO removes torque but motor may coast; Mechanical brake may be needed for vertical loads; Restart requires explicit reset procedure; Document safety function in risk assessment. Compliance: IEC 61800-5-2 (adjustable speed drives); ISO 13849 (machine safety); IEC 62061 / IEC 61508 (functional safety). The STO function must be validated as part of system safety certification.
faqs.decisionGuide: Implement dual-channel STO with hardware PWM disable; verify safety function; document in risk assessment.

faqs.question: Should I choose BLE 5.0 or Sub-1GHz for my application?
faqs.answer: Choosing between BLE 5.0 and Sub-1GHz depends on application requirements: BLE 5.0 Advantages - Smartphone connectivity: Direct connection to iOS/Android devices; Ecosystem: Large ecosystem of compatible devices; Data rate: 2Mbps for fast data transfer; Interoperability: Standard protocol ensures compatibility; Certification: Easier regulatory certification; Best for: Consumer devices, phone accessories, smart home. Sub-1GHz Advantages - Range: 2-5x longer range than 2.4GHz; Penetration: Better through walls and obstacles; Power: Lower power for same range; Spectrum: Less crowded than 2.4GHz; Cost: Lower module cost for simple applications; Best for: Industrial sensors, smart metering, long-range monitoring. Comparison: Range (urban): BLE 30-50m, Sub-1G 100-500m; Range (rural): BLE 100m, Sub-1G 1-5km; Data rate: BLE 2Mbps, Sub-1G 50-500kbps; Power (TX): BLE 4mA, Sub-1G 25mA; Power (RX): BLE 3mA, Sub-1G 10mA. Decision factors: Need smartphone connectivity -> BLE; Need maximum range -> Sub-1G; High data rate required -> BLE; Simple sensor network -> Sub-1G; Consumer product -> BLE; Industrial application -> Sub-1G. Hangshun offers both options with similar MCU features.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose BLE for smartphone connectivity and high data rate; choose Sub-1G for maximum range and penetration.

faqs.question: What is the range of Hangshun wireless MCUs?
faqs.answer: Wireless range depends on protocol, environment, and antenna design: BLE 5.0 Range - 1Mbps mode: Indoor 30-50m, Outdoor 100-150m; Coded PHY (125kbps): Indoor 100-200m, Outdoor 300-500m; Factors: 2.4GHz absorption by walls/water; Interference from WiFi/BLE devices; Antenna efficiency critical. Sub-1GHz Range - 433MHz: Urban 200-500m, Rural 1-3km; 868/915MHz: Urban 100-300m, Rural 500m-2km; Factors: Lower frequency = better penetration; Less interference than 2.4GHz; Regional regulations limit power. Range extension techniques: Higher TX power (within regulatory limits); Higher sensitivity receiver (lower dBm = better); Directional antennas (Yagi, patch); Mesh networking for multi-hop; Repeaters for coverage extension. Typical applications: Smart home (BLE): Room-level coverage; Industrial sensors (Sub-1G): Factory floor coverage; Asset tracking (BLE): Building-level; Smart metering (Sub-1G): Neighborhood-level. Antenna considerations: PCB antenna: Compact, lower efficiency; Chip antenna: Small, moderate efficiency; External antenna: Best performance, larger size. faqs.question: How do I optimize power consumption for battery-operated wireless devices?
faqs.answer: Optimizing power for wireless battery devices requires system-level approach: 1) Duty cycling - Transmit/receive only when necessary; Sleep between transmissions; Typical duty cycle: 0.1% to 1%; 2) Connection intervals - BLE: Longer intervals = lower power; Range: 7.5ms to 4s; Trade-off: Latency vs power; 3) TX power optimization - Use minimum TX power for required range; BLE: -20dBm to +8dBm programmable; 1dB reduction = ~10% power savings; 4) Sleep modes - Deep sleep with RAM retention: <1μA; Wake on timer or external interrupt; Maintain connection state in RAM; 5) Data aggregation - Collect multiple samples before transmission; Burst transmission more efficient than frequent small packets; Use notifications instead of polling. Power calculation example: Sensor reading every minute; BLE transmission: 10ms @ 5mA = 50μC; Sleep: 59.99s @ 5μA = 300μC; Average current: ~6μA; Battery life (CR2032, 225mAh): ~4 years. Additional optimizations: Reduce payload size; Use connection slave latency; Disable unused features; Optimize antenna matching. The Hangshun wireless SDK includes power profiling tools.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use long connection intervals; minimize TX power; aggregate data; deep sleep between transmissions.

faqs.question: What security features do Hangshun wireless MCUs provide?
faqs.answer: Hangshun wireless MCUs include comprehensive security features: 1) Hardware encryption - AES-128/256 hardware accelerator; 10-100x faster than software; Low power for crypto operations; 2) Secure boot - Verify firmware signature on boot; Prevent unauthorized firmware execution; Root of trust in ROM; 3) Key storage - Secure key storage in protected memory; Hardware-protected key slots; Key generation using TRNG; 4) BLE security - Pairing: Just Works, Passkey Entry, OOB; Encryption: AES-128-CCM; Privacy: Resolvable private addresses; 5) Additional features - True Random Number Generator (TRNG); CRC hardware for data integrity; Debug port disable for production; Flash read protection. Security levels: Level 1: No security (testing only); Level 2: Just Works pairing (convenience); Level 3: Passkey pairing (authenticated); Level 4: OOB or numeric comparison (high security). Implementation recommendations: Use secure boot for all production devices; Store keys in secure memory; Enable flash read protection; Use authenticated pairing for sensitive data; Implement application-layer encryption for critical data; Regular security audits and updates. Compliance: BLE security follows Bluetooth SIG specifications; FIPS 140-2 Level 1 for crypto module; Common Criteria available on request.

faeReview.author: Emily Zhao
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Wireless Applications
faeReview.content: The HS32B101C8T6 is an excellent BLE 5.0 MCU that I've used in numerous IoT projects. The 2Mbps mode is genuinely useful for firmware updates and bulk data transfer - much faster than BLE 4.2. The Coded PHY long range feature extends coverage significantly, especially in building environments. I've achieved 200m+ range in indoor tests with the 125kbps mode. The ultra-low power consumption is critical for battery applications - the <1μA sleep current with RAM retention enables years of operation on coin cells. The integrated BLE stack is mature and stable, supporting all major roles. I particularly appreciate the secure boot feature for production devices. The QFN48 package is compact but still hand-solderable for prototypes. My recommendation: Use the Coded PHY for maximum range in smart home applications, and 2Mbps for fast data transfer. The TX power adjustment helps optimize the power/range trade-off.
